expressions.dialogue: Monica: Well, go over to her! She's not with anyone. Chandler: Oh yeah, and what would my opening line be? 'Excuse me. Blarrglarrghh.' ... Oh please, **could she be more out of my league?** Ross, back me up here.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 상대방이 너무 뛰어나서 나와는 수준 차이가 너무 커 감당이 안 됨
expressions.nuance: 연애 상황에서 가장 많이 쓰이지만 직업·경쟁 상황에도 그대로 쓸 수 있음. 'way out of my league'으로 강조 가능.
expressions.example: I applied for the senior position, but honestly, they want 10 years of experience—it's way out of my league. → 시니어 포지션에 지원하긴 했는데, 솔직히 10년 경력자를 원하더라고. 내 수준이 아니야.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Out of my league. I could get a Brian. (Brian enters behind him) If I wanted to get a Brian, I could get a Brian. (Sees him) Hey, Brian.

occ.speaker: Guru Saj
occ.text: (entering) Hello, I am Guru Saj-(sees the duck)-Whoa!! (to Joey) That's supposed to be a duck right? 'Cause otherwise, this is waaay out of my league.

occ.speaker: Steve
occ.text: Oh, come on, you're way out of my league. Everybody in here knows it. Bet that guy over there's probably saying, "ooh, why she out with him? He must be rich!" Well, I'm not!

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Oh please, could she be more out of my league? **Ross, back me up here.** Ross: He could never get a woman like that in a million years. Chandler: Thank you, buddy.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 누군가의 말이나 주장을 지지하거나 동의해 주다
expressions.nuance: 의견 충돌이나 설득 상황에서 편 들어달라고 부탁할 때 씀. 'back up'은 '후진', '데이터 백업'의 의미도 있으니 문맥이 중요.
expressions.example: I'm going to tell the manager we need more time—can you back me up in the meeting? → 매니저한테 시간이 더 필요하다고 할 건데, 회의에서 내 편 좀 들어줄 수 있어?

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Whoah whoah, back up there, Sparky. What'd you mean by that?

occ.speaker: Julie
occ.text: (To Rachel) Oh, you're losin' your apron here, let me get it. There you go. (Ties it back up for her)

occ.speaker: Chan
occ.text: All right, Pheebs, back me up here, ok? You believe in that karma crap, don't you?

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Oh God, I can't believe I'm even considering this... I'm very very aware of my tongue... Ross: C'mon! C'mon! Chandler: **Here goes.** (He walks over to her but just stands there.)
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 두렵거나 결과가 불확실한 일을 막 시작하면서 하는 말 ('자, 한번 해보자')
expressions.nuance: 'Here goes nothing'은 잘 안 될 것 같다는 기대를 더 담고 있음. 보통 혼잣말처럼 쓰며 남들에게 들려주기도 함.
expressions.example: I've never spoken in front of 500 people before, but... here goes nothing. → 500명 앞에서 발표해본 적이 없는데... 자, 한번 해보자.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I think we are getting a little ahead of selves here. Okay. Okay. I am just going to get up, go to work and not think about him all day. Or else I'm just gonna get up and go to work.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on here? You go out with tons of girls.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Hi. Um, I want to start with a song thats about that moment when you suddenly realize what life is all about. OK, here we go. (plays a chord, then the lights go out) OK, thank you very much.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: ...next word... would be... Chandler! Chandler is my name, and, uh... hi. Aurora: Yes, you said that. Chandler: ...what I wanted to say was, uh... **would you like to go out with me sometime,** thankyou, goodnight.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 누군가와 데이트하다, 또는 데이트를 제안하다
expressions.nuance: 'date someone'보다 덜 직접적이고 자연스러운 표현. 이미 사귀는 사이에도 '나가서 놀다'는 의미로 쓸 수 있음.
expressions.example: I've been meaning to ask—would you like to go out with me sometime this weekend? → 물어보고 싶었는데, 이번 주말에 저랑 한번 만나볼래요?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: C'mon, you're going out with the guy! There's gotta be something wrong with him!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Wait a minute! We're talking about someone that I'm going out with?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on here? You go out with tons of girls.

expressions.dialogue: Monica: Well, go over to her! She's not with anyone. Chandler: Oh yeah, and what would my **opening line** be? 'Excuse me. Blarrglarrghh.'
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 처음 누군가에게 말을 걸 때 쓰는 첫마디, 대화를 시작하는 문장
expressions.nuance: 'pick-up line'이 이성을 유혹하는 작업 멘트라면 'opening line'은 더 일반적인 첫 인사나 대화 시작 문장을 가리킴.
expressions.example: I froze the moment I saw her—I couldn't think of a single good opening line. → 그녀를 보자마자 얼어붙었어. 첫마디를 뭐라고 해야 할지 하나도 생각이 안 나더라고.

expressions.dialogue: (The play ends and everyone applauds. As soon as the cast leaves, the gang all groan and sit down heavily.) Rachel: God. **I feel violated.** Monica: Did anybody else feel they just wanted to peel the skin off their body, to have something else to do?
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 매우 불쾌하거나 기분 나쁜 경험을 한 뒤 깊이 모욕받은 느낌이 든다
expressions.nuance: 법적 의미 없이 과장된 불쾌감을 표현하는 일상 표현. 나쁜 영화, 형편없는 서비스, 충격적인 경험 후 강하게 반응할 때 씀.
expressions.example: That ending completely ruined a two-hour movie. I feel violated. → 마지막 결말이 2시간짜리 영화를 완전히 망쳤어. 진짜 모욕감이 들어.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: I feel violated. And not in a good way.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: You can always spot someone who's never seen one of his plays before. Notice, no fear, **no sense of impending doom...** Phoebe: The exclamation point in the title scares me.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 곧 나쁜 일이 일어날 것 같은 불길한 느낌, 불안감
expressions.nuance: 'impending'은 '곧 닥칠'의 뜻으로 항상 부정적인 맥락에서만 씀. 코미디에서 과장된 불안을 표현할 때 자주 등장.
expressions.example: Every time I see an unknown number calling right before a deadline, I get this sense of impending doom. → 마감 직전에 모르는 번호로 전화가 오면 항상 뭔가 나쁜 일이 생길 것 같은 불길한 느낌이 든다.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: (staring at a woman) Ross, ten o'clock. Ross: Is it? Feels like two. Chandler: No, **ten o'clock**. Ross: What? Chandler: (sighs) **There's a beautiful woman at eight, nine, ten o'clock!**
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 시계 숫자를 방향으로 써서 손가락으로 가리키지 않고 위치를 알려주는 표현 (12시=정면, 3시=오른쪽, 9시=왼쪽)
expressions.nuance: 군대·스포츠에서 유래해 일상 대화에도 퍼진 표현. 상대가 눈치채지 않도록 조용히 방향을 알려줄 때 유용.
expressions.example: Don't look now, but there's someone staring at us—three o'clock, by the exit. → 지금 쳐다보지 마, 근데 누가 우리 보고 있어. 세 시 방향, 출구 쪽.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: It doesn't matter. I just don't want to be one of those guys that's in his office until twelve o'clock at night worrying about the WENUS.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Thanks. Listen, about the weekly numbers, I'm gonna need them on my desk by nine o'clock.

occ.speaker: Rach
occ.text: Joey, would you slow down? They're not gonna be sold out of papers at one o'clock in the morning.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Oh yeah, and what would my opening line be? 'Excuse me. Blarrglarrghh.' Rachel: Oh, c'mon. She's a person, you can do it! Chandler: Oh please, **could she be more out of my league?** Ross, back me up here.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 반어적 과장 표현으로 '더 ~할 수 있어?' = '정말 너무 ~하다'는 뜻 (Chandler의 시그니처 패턴)
expressions.nuance: Friends 전체에 걸쳐 반복되는 Chandler의 특징적인 어투. 일상에서도 유머나 과장을 나타낼 때 쓸 수 있음.
expressions.example: Could this traffic be any worse? I've been sitting here for forty minutes. → 이 차가 더 막힐 수가 있겠어? 40분째 여기 있잖아.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Couldn't be more out. (throws in cards)

occ.speaker: Russ
occ.text: You could not be more wrong. You could try... but you would not be successful.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Could not be more terrified.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: (to Joey) ...you deserve this, after all your years of struggling, you've finally been able to **crack your way into showbusiness**. Chandler: (to All) Awful play, man. Whoah.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 오랜 노력 끝에 경쟁이 치열한 분야에 간신히 진입하는 데 성공하다
expressions.nuance: 'break into'와 의미가 비슷하지만 더 구어체적이고 힘들게 비집고 들어갔다는 뉘앙스가 강함.
expressions.example: She spent years doing unpaid gigs before she finally cracked her way into the music industry. → 수년간 무급 공연을 하다가 마침내 음악 업계에 발을 들여놨어.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh no, it's terrific, it's- it's- y'know, you deserve this, after all your years of struggling, you've finally been able to crack your way into show business.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Okay, okay, fine! Make jokes, I don't care! **This is a big break for me!** Ross: You're right, you're right, it is... So you gonna invite us all to the big opening?
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 커리어에 큰 전환점이 될 수 있는 중요한 기회
expressions.nuance: 연예·스포츠·비즈니스에서 자주 쓰임. 성공을 이미 거둔 상황보다는 아직 기회가 시작되는 단계에 씀.
expressions.example: Landing a role in that Netflix show was his big break—now everyone knows his name. → 그 넷플릭스 드라마에 출연한 게 그의 대박 기회였어. 이제 모두가 그의 이름을 알잖아.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: You don't think this is going to be a big break for me?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Okay, okay, fine! Make jokes, I don't care! This is a big break for me!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Good morning, Tiger! I'm making you a nice big breakfast so you can keep up your strength for tonight. You're gonna get me good and pregnant.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: Y'know, I've done nothing but crappy plays for six years. And I finally get my shot, and **I blow it!** Monica: Maybe this wasn't your shot.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 자신의 실수나 잘못으로 중요한 기회를 망치다
expressions.nuance: 'totally', 'completely', 'really'를 앞에 붙여 정도를 강조 가능. 구어체 표현으로 격식 있는 자리에서는 'miss the opportunity'가 더 적절.
expressions.example: I finally got an interview at my dream company and completely blanked on the last question. I totally blew it. → 드디어 꿈의 회사에서 면접을 봤는데 마지막 질문에서 완전히 머릿속이 하얘졌어. 완전히 날렸어.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: (chewing gum) Ah, let's see. What next? Blow a bubble. A bubble's good. It's got a... boyish charm, it's impish. Here we go.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Right. Till I bought a blow dryer, then I was shunned.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: So far, it kinda blows. I don't know, I just thought y'know that he'd feel more like a brother y'know, like you and Ross, just like close and connected and....

expressions.dialogue: Joey: ...I finally **get my shot**, and I blow it! Ross: Yeah, I mean... I think when **it's your shot**, y'know, you-you know it's your shot. Did it... feel like your shot? Joey: Hard to tell, I was naked.
expressions.speaker: Joey / Ross
expressions.meaning: 중요한 무언가에 드디어 진짜 기회를 얻다
expressions.nuance: 'shot'을 기회의 의미로 쓰는 표현. 한 번뿐인 소중한 기회 느낌. 'give it your best shot'(최선을 다하다)과 구별할 것.
expressions.example: I've been waiting three years for this promotion. This is finally my shot—I'm not going to waste it. → 3년을 기다렸어. 드디어 내 차례야. 절대 날리지 않을 거야.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Yeah, he's just gotta get a shot.

occ.speaker: Mrs. Geller
occ.text: Get a shot of Monica. Where's Monica.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (running into the hall, in slow motion) You'll never get me, Joe!!! (he then pretends that he gets shot repeatedly and falls back against Joey and Chandler's door, dead.)

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: ...it's like I'm two guys. One guy's going 'Shut up! This is great!' But there's this other guy—the same guy that **wells up every time** that Grinch's heart grows three sizes and breaks that measuring device...
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 눈물이나 감정이 북받쳐 오르다 (실제로 울지는 않더라도)
expressions.nuance: 'tear up'과 비슷하지만 더 은유적. 남성이 감정을 수줍게 표현할 때 자주 씀. 눈물이 차오르는 단계이지 흘리는 단계는 아님.
expressions.example: I always well up at the end of that film, even though I've seen it a dozen times. → 그 영화 열두 번이나 봤는데도 마지막에는 항상 눈물이 맺혀.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (welling up) Mm-hmm.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Well, don't we look nice all dressed up?...It's stuff like that, isn't it?

occ.speaker: Janice
occ.text: Well then ask yourself this. Why do you think we keep ending up together? New Year's? Who invited who? Valentine's? Who asked who into whose bed?

expressions.dialogue: Aurora: Tell me one of your stories. Chandler: Alright. Once I got on the subway, right, and it was at night, and I rode it all the way to Brooklyn... **just for the hell of it.**
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 특별한 이유 없이, 그냥 재미로 또는 충동적으로
expressions.nuance: 'for the fun of it'보다 약간 더 캐주얼하고 구어체적. 격식 있는 자리에서는 피하는 게 좋음. 'just'를 앞에 붙이는 경우가 많음.
expressions.example: I wasn't even hungry, but I ordered a second dessert just for the hell of it. → 배도 안 고팠는데 그냥 디저트를 하나 더 시켰어.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: We talked 'til like two. It was this perfect evening... **more or less.** (Aurora's story about her husband then emerges.)
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 대략, 거의, 완전하지는 않지만 그 정도라고 볼 수 있다
expressions.nuance: 문장 끝에 붙여 '완전히 그렇지는 않은데 대체로 그렇다'는 뉘앙스를 부드럽게 추가할 수 있음. 불확실하거나 완전히 동의하지 않을 때도 씀.
expressions.example: Did the presentation go well? — Yeah, more or less. There were a couple of tough questions. → 발표 잘 됐어? — 뭐, 어느 정도. 까다로운 질문이 몇 개 있긴 했어.

expressions.dialogue: Director: And cut. What was that? Joey: **I was going for** quiet desperation. But if you have to ask... Director: I think his butt would like to get this shot before lunch.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 특정 분위기, 스타일, 효과를 노리거나 의도하다
expressions.nuance: 연기·패션·디자인·글쓰기 등에서 의도한 방향을 설명할 때 씀. 'What are you going for?'는 '어떤 느낌을 노린 거야?'라는 뜻.
expressions.example: I was going for a minimalist look with this poster, but now I think it's just... empty. → 이 포스터에서 미니멀한 느낌을 노렸는데, 이제 보니 그냥 비어 보이는 것 같아.

occ.speaker: Barry
occ.text: Yeah! It's fine, it's fine. Robbie's gonna be here for hours.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: (leaving to serve others) Good for me!

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Okay, okay. If I were omnipotent for a day, I would want, um, world peace, no more hunger, good things for the rain-forest...And bigger boobs!

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Thank God you didn't try to fan out the magazines. I mean, **she'll scratch your eyes right out.** Monica: You guys, I am not that bad!
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 매우 화난 사람이 격렬하게 공격할 것이라는 과장된 표현
expressions.nuance: 실제 폭력을 뜻하지 않으며 보통 유머 섞인 경고로 씀. 'right out'을 붙이면 더 강조됨.
expressions.example: Do NOT touch the last slice of her birthday cake. She will scratch your eyes out. → 그녀의 생일 케이크 마지막 조각에 손대지 마. 눈 파낼 거야.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: Thank God you didn't try to **fan out** the magazines. I mean, she'll scratch your eyes right out. Monica: You guys, I am not that bad!
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 물건을 부채꼴 모양으로 펼치다; 또는 여러 방향으로 흩어지다
expressions.nuance: 잡지·카드 등을 보기 좋게 부채꼴로 배열하는 것. 또는 사람들이 여러 방향으로 나뉘어 퍼지는 것도 표현 가능.
expressions.example: Fan out the brochures on the welcome table so guests can pick them up easily. → 안내 책자를 환영 테이블에 부채꼴로 펼쳐놔서 손님들이 쉽게 가져갈 수 있게 해.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Fan out! Fan out!

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: I don't know. Ooh! Ooh! Look it! (She fans out the trick deck.) Ah-ha!

occ.speaker: The Fan
occ.text: Can-can I get your autograph, I'm your biggest fan. (Holds out a napkin and a pen.)

expressions.dialogue: Rachel: Uh, Mon, you-you gonna leave your shoes out here? Monica: (determined) Uh-huh! Rachel: Really? Just **casually strewn about** in that reckless haphazard manner?
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 물건들이 아무렇게나 여기저기 흩어져 있다
expressions.nuance: 구어체보다는 약간 격식 있는 느낌. 글쓰기에서도 자주 쓰임. Rachel이 여기선 과장된 묘사로 Monica를 놀리는 맥락.
expressions.example: I walked into his apartment and there were clothes strewn about everywhere—no wonder he couldn't find his keys. → 그의 아파트에 들어갔더니 옷이 사방에 아무렇게나 흩어져 있었어. 열쇠를 못 찾는 게 당연하지.

expressions.dialogue: Monica: Oh. I'm sorry it didn't work out. Chandler: What 'not work out'? I'm seeing her again on Thursday! Monica: I mean, this is twisted! **How could you get involved with a woman like this?**
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 누군가와 연애 관계를 시작하다; 복잡한 상황이나 사람에게 얽혀들다
expressions.nuance: 연애 외에도 복잡하거나 문제가 있는 상황에 발을 들일 때도 씀. 걱정이나 의구심이 담긴 경우가 많음.
expressions.example: I warned him not to get involved with anyone from the same project team, but he didn't listen. → 같은 팀 사람이랑 엮이지 말라고 경고했는데 듣질 않았어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: But-but you know you cannot get involved with your assistant.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: See, this is why I told you never get involved with your assistant! And here is no such thing as keeping secrets when it comes to affairs. (To Chandler) Did you hear that Chandler? No such thing!

expressions.dialogue: Aurora: Why can't we just talk, and laugh, and make love, without feeling obligated to one another... and **up until tonight** I thought that's what you wanted too.
expressions.speaker: Aurora
expressions.meaning: 특정 시점까지는 (그랬다는 것을 강조하며)
expressions.nuance: 'until'보다 강조적. 그 시점까지는 그 상태였다가 이제 변화가 생겼다는 뉘앙스를 더 명확하게 전달.
expressions.example: Up until last month, everything was on track. Then the whole timeline got pushed back. → 지난달까지만 해도 다 계획대로 가고 있었어. 그러다 갑자기 전체 일정이 뒤로 밀렸어.

occ.speaker: Mrs. Bing
occ.text: No. Because I know how to write men that women fall in love with. Believe me, I cannot sell a Paolo. People will not turn three hundred twenty-five pages for a Paolo. C'mon, the guy's a secondary character, a, y'know, complication you eventually kill off.

occ.speaker: Malcom
occ.text: (reading) I met Phoebe today. She was really nice to me eventhough I'm such a loser. And, then when I was walking home I thought about her a lot, it was weird, but kinda cool.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Yeah, either that, or Gloria Estefan was right, eventually, the rhythm is going to get you.

expressions.dialogue: Joey: The Estelle Leonard Talent Agency. Wow, an agency left me its card! Maybe they **wanna sign me!** Phoebe: Based on this play? ...Based on this play!
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 에이전시나 팀이 누군가를 공식 계약하다, 소속시키다
expressions.nuance: 연예계·스포츠에서 에이전트·소속사·구단이 계약을 맺을 때 씀. 일반 취업과 달리 개인과 에이전시 간의 전속 계약 관계.
expressions.example: She posted a cover online and a record label reached out to sign her within a week. → 그녀가 커버곡을 올렸더니 일주일 만에 레코드 레이블에서 계약하자고 연락이 왔어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: (overemphasizing) Mmmmmm! Everything smells so delicious! You know, I can't remember a time I smelt such a delicious combination of (Monica signals her to stop) of, OK, smells.

occ.speaker: Fran
occ.text: Look, you're cold, I have to pee, and... (indicating the sign) ..there's a cup of coffee on the window. How bad could it be?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: We forgot to sign one of the admissions forms.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: (to Joey) ...y'know, **you deserve this**, after all your years of struggling, you've finally been able to crack your way into showbusiness. (to All) Awful play, man. Whoah.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 오랜 노력이나 고생 끝에 얻은 것이니 충분히 받을 자격이 있다
expressions.nuance: 격려와 축하를 동시에 담은 표현. 노력이 보상받는 상황에서 씀. 반대로 나쁜 일이 생겼을 때 비꼬는 표현으로도 쓰임 (문맥에 주의).
expressions.example: You've put in six months of extra work on this project. You deserve this promotion. → 6개월 동안 이 프로젝트에 추가로 매달렸잖아. 이번 승진은 당연히 받을 자격 있어.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: I know no, no, y'know you don't deserve this, you don't Ross. You're, you're really, you're so good. (kisses him on the cheek)

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh no, it's terrific, it's- it's- y'know, you deserve this, after all your years of struggling, you've finally been able to crack your way into show business.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: ...could she be more out of my league? Ross, back me up here. Ross: **He could never get a woman like that in a million years.** Chandler: Thank you, buddy.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 절대로, 어떤 상황에서도 그럴 리 없다 (강한 부정의 과장)
expressions.nuance: 'never in a million years'도 가능. 불가능한 일을 단호하게 강조할 때 씀. 'never'를 쓰는 것보다 훨씬 극적인 표현.
expressions.example: Would I ever move back to my hometown? Not in a million years. → 내가 고향으로 다시 돌아가겠냐고? 절대, 평생.
